name: Haloalkanes and Haloarenes
subject: Chemistry

sections.heading: Classification and Nomenclature
sections.body:
Compounds containing C–X (X = F, Cl, Br, I) are called haloalkanes (alkyl halides) when X is on a sp³ carbon, and haloarenes (aryl halides) when X is on an sp² carbon of an aromatic ring.

By the nature of the carbon bearing X:

  • Primary (1°): X on a carbon attached to one other C — CH3CH2Cl.
  • Secondary (2°): X on a carbon attached to two other Cs — (CH3)2CHCl.
  • Tertiary (3°): X on a carbon attached to three other Cs — (CH3)3CCl.

By the type of hybridisation of C bearing X:

  • Allylic: X on sp³ C adjacent to C=C (CH2=CH–CH2X).
  • Benzylic: X on sp³ C adjacent to aromatic ring (C6H5–CH2X).
  • Vinylic: X on sp² C of C=C (CH2=CHX).
  • Aryl: X on sp² C of arene (C6H5X).

IUPAC: longest chain containing the C–X is parent; X is named as a halo substituent and gets the lowest locant. Example: (CH3)2CHCH(Br)CH3 = 2-bromo-3-methylbutane.

sections.heading: Methods of Preparation
sections.body:
From alcohols:

  • R–OH + HX → R–X + H2O. Reactivity: HI > HBr > HCl. Tertiary alcohols react with HCl alone; primary need ZnCl2 + HCl (Lucas test).
  • With PCl5: R–OH + PCl5 → R–Cl + POCl3 + HCl.
  • With PCl3: 3 R–OH + PCl3 → 3 R–Cl + H3PO3.
  • With SOCl2 (Darzens method): R–OH + SOCl2 → R–Cl + SO2↑ + HCl↑. Best because byproducts are gases — pure halide.

From hydrocarbons: free-radical halogenation of alkanes (poor selectivity, mixtures).

From alkenes: addition of HX (Markovnikov) or X2; in presence of peroxides, HBr addition is anti-Markovnikov (Kharasch effect).

Halide exchange (Finkelstein reaction): R–Cl/Br + NaI →acetone R–I + NaCl/NaBr↓ (NaCl/NaBr precipitate in acetone drives equilibrium).

Swarts reaction: R–X + AgF (or Hg2F2, CoF2) → R–F + AgX.

Haloarenes: from arenes by electrophilic substitution with X2/FeX3; from diazonium salts (Sandmeyer: ArN2⁺ + CuCl/CuBr → ArCl/ArBr; Gattermann uses Cu/HX; Hunsdiecker would be... not on syllabus here).

sections.heading: Physical Properties
sections.body:
State: Lower haloalkanes are gases or liquids (e.g. CH3Cl is gas), higher members are liquids or solids.

Boiling point: increases with molecular size (more London forces). For same alkyl group, b.p. order: R–I > R–Br > R–Cl > R–F (more electrons, larger polarisability). For isomers, b.p. decreases with branching (less surface contact). Haloarenes are liquid/solid; their b.p. values are higher than corresponding haloalkanes.

Density: increases C–F < C–Cl < C–Br < C–I. Bromides, iodides, polychlorides denser than water.

Solubility: very slightly soluble in water (cannot form H-bonds with water) but soluble in organic solvents.

sections.heading: Nucleophilic Substitution Reactions — SN1 vs SN2
sections.body:
Haloalkanes undergo nucleophilic substitution: R–X + Nu⁻ → R–Nu + X⁻. Two mechanisms:

SN2 (bimolecular): rate = k[R–X][Nu]. Single-step, concerted; Nu attacks the carbon from the side opposite to X (back-side attack). Transition state has Nu and X partially bonded; results in inversion of configuration (Walden inversion).

Order of reactivity for SN2: CH3X > 1° > 2° > 3° (steric crowding hinders backside attack).

SN1 (unimolecular): rate = k[R–X]. Two-step: (i) slow ionisation R–X → R⁺ + X⁻ giving a carbocation; (ii) fast attack by Nu. Carbocation is planar (sp²), so Nu can attack from either face → racemisation (with some inversion preference).

Order of reactivity for SN1: 3° > 2° > 1° > CH3X (more substituted carbocation is more stable; allyl/benzyl also fast).

Solvent effects: polar protic solvents (water, alcohols) favour SN1 (stabilise cation and anion). Polar aprotic solvents (acetone, DMSO, DMF) favour SN2 (do not solvate Nu, leaving it more reactive).

Leaving group: weaker bases are better leaving groups: I⁻ > Br⁻ > Cl⁻ > F⁻.

Allyl and benzyl halides: undergo SN1 readily because the resulting carbocations are resonance-stabilised. They also undergo SN2 readily because the C–X carbon is sp³ but adjacent π system stabilises the transition state.

sections.heading: Elimination Reactions and Saytzeff Rule
sections.body:
With strong bases (e.g. KOH/alc.), haloalkanes undergo β-elimination (E2) to give alkenes:

CH3–CH2–CH(Br)–CH3 + KOH (alc) → mostly 2-butene + minor 1-butene.

Saytzeff (Zaitsev) rule: in dehydrohalogenation, the alkene with the more substituted (more stable) double bond is the major product.

Substitution vs elimination depends on:

  • Substrate: 3° favours elimination; 1° favours substitution.
  • Base/Nu: bulky strong bases (e.g. tBuO⁻) favour E; small good Nu (CN⁻, I⁻) favour S.
  • Temperature: high T favours E (gain of entropy).
  • Solvent: alc. KOH → E; aq. KOH → S (gives alcohol).

sections.heading: Reactions with Metals — Grignard and Wurtz
sections.body:
Grignard reagent: R–X + Mg →dry ether R–Mg–X. Highly reactive; the C–Mg bond is strongly polar (C is δ−). Used to build C–C bonds with carbonyl, CO2, epoxides, etc. Must avoid water/H-source — even traces of moisture decompose Grignard to alkane.

Wurtz reaction: 2 R–X + 2 Na →dry ether R–R + 2 NaX. Couples two alkyl halides to a symmetrical alkane (poor for unsymmetrical).

Wurtz–Fittig (haloarene + haloalkane + Na): Ar–X + R–X + 2 Na → Ar–R + 2 NaX. Fittig: 2 Ar–X + 2 Na → Ar–Ar + 2 NaX (biaryl).

sections.heading: Reactivity of Haloarenes (Aryl Halides)
sections.body:
Haloarenes are much less reactive than haloalkanes towards nucleophilic substitution because:

  1. The C–X bond has partial double-bond character due to resonance of lone pair on X with the ring (shorter, stronger).
  2. The sp² carbon has higher electronegativity than sp³, holding electrons of C–X more tightly.
  3. Approach of Nu is hindered by the π electron cloud of the ring.
  4. The phenyl cation is unstable, so SN1 is unfavourable; aryl carbanion intermediate of SN2 is also unstable.

Substitution becomes feasible if strong electron-withdrawing groups (–NO2, –CN) are at ortho/para positions (resonance stabilisation of intermediate). Example: 2,4,6-trinitrochlorobenzene reacts with NaOH at warm conditions.

Electrophilic substitution on haloarenes: halogens are slightly deactivating but ortho/para directing (lone pair donates by resonance, sigma withdraws by induction; net effect is weakly deactivating but o/p directing).

sections.heading: Polyhalogen Compounds and Environmental Effects
sections.body:
Important polyhalogen compounds:

  • Dichloromethane (CH2Cl2): paint remover, propellant in aerosols. Harmful in high concentrations.
  • Chloroform (CHCl3): was used as anaesthetic; now a solvent. Slowly oxidised in air/light to phosgene COCl2 (highly toxic) — stored in dark bottles with a little ethanol.
  • Iodoform (CHI3): yellow solid, antiseptic (action due to release of free I2).
  • Carbon tetrachloride (CCl4): was in fire extinguishers (Pyrene); causes liver cancer; depletes ozone.
  • Freons (CFCl3, CF2Cl2 etc.): refrigerants, aerosol propellants. Major cause of ozone-layer depletion (release Cl radicals catalysing O3 destruction).
  • DDT (p,p'-dichlorodiphenyltrichloroethane): first synthetic insecticide; non-biodegradable, accumulates in food chain — banned in many countries.

summary: Haloalkanes (R–X) and haloarenes (Ar–X) are alkyl/aryl derivatives in which a halogen replaces hydrogen. Their preparation routes include alcohol substitution (HX, PX_3, PCl_5, SOCl_2 — best because byproducts are gases), free-radical halogenation, addition to alkenes, Finkelstein, Swarts, and Sandmeyer for arenes. C–X is polar so nucleophilic substitution (SN1 vs SN2) and elimination (E1/E2) dominate. SN2 follows CH_3 > 1° > 2° > 3° with backside attack and Walden inversion; SN1 follows 3° > 2° > 1° via planar carbocation, giving racemisation. Polar protic solvents favour SN1; polar aprotic favour SN2. Saytzeff rule predicts the more-substituted alkene as the major elimination product. Aryl halides are inert to Nu substitution unless electron-withdrawing groups (especially −NO_2 at o/p) activate the ring. Polyhalogen compounds (CHCl_3, CCl_4, freons, DDT) raise serious environmental and health concerns.
pitfalls: Forgetting Walden inversion in SN2 — stereochemistry matters in MCQs.
Predicting SN2 for tertiary substrates — they overwhelmingly go SN1 / E1.
Using aqueous KOH expecting alkene (it gives alcohol, SN); use alcoholic KOH for elimination.
Saying haloarenes undergo facile SN — they don't, except with strong activation by ortho/para −NO_2.
Forgetting Markovnikov vs anti-Markovnikov for HBr addition (peroxide effect only with HBr, not HCl/HI).
Mis-ordering SN2 reactivity — it's CH_3X > 1° > 2° > 3°, opposite of SN1.
Using halogen as activator in EAS — halogens are deactivators (but o/p directors).
